Lead Quality Optimization — Progress Report

Leisure Pools Houston, Austin, Dallas Fort Worth, San Antonio, Waco, OK
Thursday, March 19, 2026 Changes Implemented: March 12, 2026 Comparison: Mar 12–18 vs Mar 5–11

1. Changes Implemented — March 12, 2026

We implemented a series of changes to improve lead quality, filter out unqualified traffic, and focus ad spend on homeowners actively researching fiberglass pool installation.

What We Changed

All Campaigns (Houston, DFW, Austin, San Antonio, Waco, OK)
  • Excluded ages 18–24
  • Excluded household income tiers: 41–50th percentile and Lower 50%
  • Added tighter negative keyword lists to filter irrelevant searches
  • Added audience segments in observation mode (homeowners, education, employment industry, in-market pools & spas) to collect data for future bid adjustments
Houston Only — Additional Changes
  • Raised max CPC bid caps: Primary campaign $15 → $40, Secondary campaign $15 → $25
  • Restructured keywords around higher-intent, fiberglass-specific search terms
  • Tightened match types (broad → phrase/exact) to control which searches trigger ads
  • Added cross-campaign negative keywords to prevent internal competition
With more aggressive targeting of higher-intent, higher-budget searchers, you'd naturally expect impressions and clicks to fall and cost per click to rise — we're going from casting a very wide net to a very narrow one. We're removing unqualified impressions and concentrating the budget on relevant searches.

2. Houston — Search Impression Share Growth

16.5% → 29.5%
Account-Wide Search Impression Share
What this means: Before the changes, our ads were appearing in roughly 1 in 6 relevant searches across the Houston market. After one week, we're showing in nearly 1 in 3. The same budget is reaching a significantly larger share of the searches that matter.
Fiberglass Pools campaign specifically: 15.6% → 53.3% impression share — more than 3x the visibility on our core fiberglass search terms.

The spend is essentially the same, but we're paying more per click for higher-intent, more relevant traffic. Fewer clicks, but better clicks.

3. Search Quality Improvement — Houston (Fiberglass)

Side-by-side comparison of top search terms by spend — before and after changes.

Before (Mar 5–11) — Top Spend Terms

  • fiberglass pools $63.46
  • fiberglass pool $11.88

After (Mar 12–18) — Top Spend Terms

  • fiberglass pool cost $4.67
Key takeaway: The "After" column is almost entirely composed of relevant, purchase-intent search terms. The irrelevant traffic — pool stores, competitors, DIY, wrong product types — that was consuming budget has been eliminated.

Fiberglass already had a strong foundation. The broader pool-builder and pool-company campaigns are where we're now working to capture searchers who may not know about fiberglass yet but are actively shopping for a pool.

4. Demographic Targeting & Audience Insights — Houston

We're directing traffic toward the highest-value audience segments. Here's what the data shows after one week.

Household Income — Post-Change Traffic Distribution

Conversions are now concentrated in the Top 10% household income tier. Even though total lead volume is down, we have strong reason to believe we're reaching the right people.

Age Distribution

Current Demographic Exclusions (All Campaigns)

Audience Segments — Observation Mode

We're actively collecting performance data on these audience segments across all ad groups. Once we have enough signal, we can adjust bids to prioritize the segments that convert best.

Audience Profile — Who's Clicking

Pools & Spas in-market audience: 121.9x index, 27.2% of clicks. The traffic we're paying for is overwhelmingly composed of people actively researching pool purchases.

Fiberglass Pools: Already had a strong foundation — impression share more than tripled and CTR improved by 4+ points. The Pool Builder / Contractor campaign is where we're working to capture broader pool-buying searches from people who may not know about fiberglass yet.

Google's own Performance Insights attributes the click decrease to "Targeting changes" — confirming the drop is a direct result of our intentional tightening.

7. Next Steps

  • 1
    Relearning period complete — more data incoming
    The keyword and bid cap changes triggered a ~5 day relearning period for Google's algorithms. That window just ended. Weekends tend to carry the highest pool search volume, so we expect the data going forward to be increasingly representative of the new campaign structure.
  • 2
    We'll rely on feedback from the Pools 123 team to evaluate lead quality
    It's too early to see conversion volume changes, but we'd like to hear if the team has noticed any patterns in the calls and leads coming through — particularly late last week and the weekend through yesterday for Houston. That feedback will help us calibrate whether to hold, push further, or adjust.
  • 3
    Apply Houston learnings to other markets
    Houston received the most aggressive changes. DFW, Austin, San Antonio, Waco, and Oklahoma received demographic exclusions and negative keywords. Based on what we see from Houston, we're prepared to make the same keyword restructuring and bid cap adjustments to the other markets.
  • 4
    Audience-based bid optimization
    We're currently observing audience segments (homeowners, income tiers, employment industries, in-market pools & spas). As data accumulates, we can begin automating bid adjustments — for example, increasing bid caps 20–30% for the Top 10% household income tier and users in the Pools & Spas in-market segment — to further prioritize the highest-value searchers.
  • 5
    Ongoing search term monitoring
    We'll continue reviewing search terms weekly and adding exclusions as needed to keep the traffic clean.
